perturbation.original_text: 1.2 Non-Competition . For a period of three (3) years from and after the Effective Date (the “ Restrictive Period ”), Sellers shall not engage, directly or indirectly, through any other entity or with any person (whether as an owner, shareholder, partner, member, director, officer, employee, agent, investor, or otherwise), in any business activity which is in competition with the Business conducted by Buyer during the Restrictive Period.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.2 Non-Competition . For a period of ten (10) years from and after the Effective Date (the “ Restrictive Period ”), Sellers shall not engage, directly or indirectly, through any other entity or with any person (whether as an owner, shareholder, partner, member, director, officer, employee, agent, investor, or otherwise), in any business activity which is in competition with the Business conducted by Buyer during the Restrictive Period.
perturbation.explanation: The modification changes the non-compete period from three years to ten years, which may be deemed unreasonably long and therefore unenforceable under Colorado law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Colorado law states that covenants not to compete are generally disfavored but can be enforceable if they are for a reasonable duration and scope. A ten-year non-compete period is likely to be considered unreasonable, especially given the specific industry and the positions of the parties involved, and thus violates C.R.S. § 8-2-113.
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the non-compete period from three years to ten years. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation suggest that a ten-year non-compete period is likely unreasonable under Colorado law. Although there are no scraped snippets available to support this claim, the modified text creates a legal contradiction by implementing a term that might be unenforceable under state law, based on the provided explanations.

perturbation.original_text: Notwithstanding the foregoing, Sellers may, during the 60-day period following the Effective Date, provide services in the Business to those customers delineated on Schedule 1.02(c) of the Purchase Agreement, subject to and in accordance with Section 5.08 of the Purchase Agreement; provided such services shall be provided solely pursuant to the customer contracts listed on such Schedule 1.02(c) and the amount of such service shall not be materially greater than that provided to such customers by Sellers during the 60-day period prior to the Effective Date.
perturbation.changed_text: Notwithstanding the foregoing, Sellers may, during the 180-day period following the Effective Date, provide services in the Business to those customers delineated on Schedule 1.02(c) of the Purchase Agreement, subject to and in accordance with Section 5.08 of the Purchase Agreement; provided such services shall not be subject to any limitations or regulations, including those set forth by federal regulatory bodies.
perturbation.explanation: The modification allows the sellers to provide services without limitations imposed by federal regulatory bodies which contradicts the state and federal regulations about telecommunications services.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Communications Act of 1934, as amended, 47 U.S.C. § 251
perturbation.law_explanation: By stating that the services provided during the 180-day period are not subject to any limitations or regulations including those of federal regulatory bodies, the modified text directly contradicts the Communications Act of 1934 which establishes regulatory oversight of telecommunications services. All providers including these sellers are bound by federal laws governing telecommunications.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 47 U.S. Code § 251 - Interconnection | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 47 U.S. Code § 251 - Interconnection U.S. Code Notes Authorities (CFR) prev | next (a) General duty of telecommunications carriers Each telecommunications carrier has the duty— (1) to interconnect directly or indirectly with the facilities and equipment of other telecommunications carriers ; and (2) not to install network features, functions, or capabilities that do not comply with the guidelines and standards established pursuant to section 255 or 256 of this title. (b) Obligations of all local exchange carriers Each local exchange carrier has the following duties: (1) Resale The duty not to prohibit, and not to impose unreasonable or discriminatory conditions or limitations on, the resale of its telecommunications services . (2) Number portability The duty to provide, to the extent technically feasible, number portability in accordance with requirements prescribed by the Commission. (3) Dialing parity The duty to provide dialing parity to competing providers of telephone exchange service and telephone toll service, and the duty to permit all such providers to have nondiscriminatory access to telephone numbers, operator services, directory assistance, and directory listing, with no unreasonable dialing delays. (4) Access to rights-of-way The duty to afford access to the poles, ducts, conduits, and rights-of-way of such carrier to competing providers of telecommunications services on rates, terms, and conditions that are consistent with section 224 of this title . (5) Reciprocal compensation The duty to establish reciprocal compensation arrangements for the transport and termination of telecommunications . perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly states that services provided during the 180-day period are 'not subject to any limitations or regulations, including those set forth by federal regulatory bodies.' This directly contradicts the Communications Act of 1934 (as supported by the Law Explanation) which establishes regulatory oversight of telecommunications services. Snippet 1 discusses the general duty of telecommunications carriers and Snippet 2 alludes to regulations, but without enough context. The removal of regulatory oversight creates a direct contradiction with the implicit regulations suggested in the snippets and made explicit in the Law Explanation, as all providers, including the sellers, are bound by federal telecommunications laws.

perturbation.original_text: 1.3 Non-Solicitation . During the Restrictive Period, without the prior written consent of the Buyer (which Buyer may withhold in the exercise of its sole, absolute, and arbitrary discretion), Sellers (including, without limitation, any successors) shall not induce or solicit, directly or indirectly, through any other entity or with any other person, (whether as an owner, shareholder, partner, member, director, officer, employee, agent, investor, or otherwise) any employee of the Buyer or its Affiliates who is involved in the managed modem business conducted by Buyer or any of its Affiliates, including, without limitation, the Business, to terminate the employee’s employment with the Buyer or any such Affiliate.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.3 Non-Solicitation . During the Restrictive Period, Sellers (including, without limitation, any successors) shall not induce or solicit, directly or indirectly, through any other entity or with any other person, (whether as an owner, shareholder, partner, member, director, officer, employee, agent, investor, or otherwise) any employee of the Buyer or its Affiliates who is involved in the managed modem business conducted by Buyer or any of its Affiliates, including, without limitation, the Business, to terminate the employee’s employment with the Buyer or any such Affiliate. This clause will be held regardless of employee's legal labor protections to seek new employment.
perturbation.explanation: The modification removes the requirement for the Buyer's consent and adds a provision disregarding employee's labor protections, which is a violation of the National Labor Relations Act.
perturbation.law_explanation: By stating that the non-solicitation clause will be upheld regardless of an employee's legal labor protections to seek new employment, the modified text violates Section 7 of the National Labor Relations Act (NLRA), which guarantees employees the right to self-organization, to form, join, or assist labor organizations, to bargain collectively through representatives of their own choosing, and to engage in other concerted activities for the purpose of collective bargaining or other mutual aid or protection.

perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly states that the non-solicitation clause will be upheld regardless of employee's legal labor protections to seek new employment. This directly contradicts the employee's right to seek new employment, thus conflicting with labor laws. The original text required the Buyer's consent, offering some protection, but the changed text removes this and further invalidates any existing legal protection, creating a legal contradiction falling under the definition of 'Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction'. While both law snippets are unavailable, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both state that the change violates employee's legal rights.

perturbation.original_text: 2.3 Applicable Law, Jurisdiction, and Venue . This Agreement shall be governed by, and construed in accordance with, the Laws of the State of Colorado applicable to agreements made and to be performed wholly within such jurisdiction. The parties hereby irrevocably and unconditionally consent to the exclusive jurisdiction of the courts of the State of Colorado and of the United States of America, in each case located in the County of Denver, for any litigation arising out of or relating to this Agreement, and further agree that service of any process, summons, notice or document by U.S. registered mail to its respective address set forth in this Agreement shall be effective service of process for any litigation brought against it in any such court.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.3 Applicable Law, Jurisdiction, and Venue . This Agreement shall be governed by, and construed in accordance with, the Laws of the State of Delaware, regardless of where the agreement is performed. The parties hereby irrevocably and unconditionally consent to the exclusive jurisdiction of the courts of the State of Delaware for any litigation arising out of or relating to this Agreement, and further agree that service of any process, summons, notice or document by U.S. registered mail to its respective address set forth in this Agreement shall be effective service of process for any litigation brought against it in any such court.
perturbation.explanation: The modification changes the governing law from Colorado to Delaware, despite the contract being performed in Colorado, which could conflict with Colorado's choice of law rules and statutes that favor Colorado law when the contract is substantially performed there. Further, the change implies the elimination of US Federal courts.
perturbation.law_explanation: By changing the governing law to Delaware, the modified text potentially contradicts Colorado Revised Statutes § 13-1-124, which addresses choice of law in certain circumstances, favoring Colorado law if the agreement is substantially performed in Colorado. This statute reflects Colorado's interest in applying its own laws to contracts performed within its borders. Also, removing the Federal courts is problematic as the contract touches upon interstate commerce.
perturbation.justification: The changed text alters the governing law from Colorado to Delaware, potentially contradicting Colorado Revised Statutes § 13-1-124, which may favor Colorado law if the agreement is substantially performed in Colorado, as stated in the Law Explanation. This shift undermines Colorado's interest in applying its own laws to contracts performed within its borders. Furthermore, while there are no scraped snippets to directly confirm this, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation indicate that the elimination of US Federal Courts from the jurisdiction clause is also problematic. Given the lack of explicit law snippets, the determination is based on the consistency of the Perturbation and Law Explanations which point to a likely legal contradiction.
